title: Can China create the next Apple or Nvidia?
gist: As China's manufacturing share of GDP shrinks, Henry Storey argues Beijing is pivoting industrial policy toward high-margin 'producer services' (design, IP, software, R&D) to capture more of the value chain like Apple and Nvidia do, but China's security-first, vertically-integrated economic culture and its usual subsidy-driven policy toolkit are poorly suited to replicating that model.

insights.claim: China's State Council called in April 2026 for the services sector to prioritise providing manufacturers with logistics, supply chain finance, R&D design, software and data/IT services — a recalibration after Beijing had removed specific services-sector targets from five-year plans in 2020.
insights.speaker: Henry Storey
insights.so_what: A concrete policy pivot to track: Chinese producer-services firms (design, R&D, software) may become a new focus of state industrial support alongside traditional manufacturing.
insights.locator: the State Council in April specifically called on the services sector

insights.claim: China's manufacturing share of GDP has fallen from around 30% in 2017 to just below 25% in 2024, despite anemic consumption and gargantuan state support for producers, showing China has been unable to defy the general trend of manufacturing's relative economic decline as economies mature.
insights.speaker: Henry Storey
insights.so_what: A hard data point undercutting the narrative of China's manufacturing share holding steady — structural rebalancing toward services is already underway despite heavy state intervention.
insights.locator: manufacturing's share of GDP has fallen

insights.claim: Beijing's 2020 removal of services-sector targets and its current pivot back toward services was shaped by watching deindustrialisation's political toll on the West from 2015-2020, and by the vulnerability exposed by export controls on Huawei and the first Sino-US trade war.
insights.speaker: Henry Storey
insights.so_what: Frames China's producer-services push as partly a defensive response to US tech restrictions, not purely an economic maturation story — implies further escalation in export controls could accelerate the pivot.

themes.theme: Why: profit capture and job creation

insights.claim: Apple produces less than 20% of global mobile phones but captures around 80% of the industry's overall profits, while Chinese suppliers accounting for roughly a quarter of the iPhone's bill of materials compete on wafer-thin margins — illustrating why Beijing wants a bigger share of high-value producer services rather than just assembly.
insights.speaker: Henry Storey
insights.so_what: Quantifies the value-capture gap Beijing is trying to close; a benchmark for how much upside exists if Chinese firms move up the value chain into design/IP.

insights.claim: Beijing sees producer services as integral to job creation, which remains politically salient given stubbornly high youth unemployment and the rapid automation of manufacturing, and as a new growth driver as traditional growth pillars face exhaustion.
insights.speaker: Henry Storey
insights.so_what: Ties the producer-services push directly to domestic political pressure (youth unemployment) rather than purely export competitiveness.
insights.locator: Beijing also sees producer services as being integral to job creation

insights.claim: Chinese manufacturers including BYD (electric vehicles), Huawei (telecoms, semiconductors, self-driving vehicles), Xiaomi (smartphones, EVs, appliances) and WuXi AppTec (contract R&D and life-sciences manufacturing) already have globally competitive R&D, design and software capabilities.
insights.speaker: Henry Storey
insights.so_what: Identifies specific Chinese companies already succeeding in the producer-services shift — a starting watchlist for where China's value-chain upgrade is real, not aspirational.

insights.claim: Companies providing research, tech and software services have recently recorded the fastest growth of any sector among entrants to China's 'Little Giants' program, which develops SMEs with world-leading capabilities in niche markets, while China's exports of knowledge-intensive activities have also grown appreciably.
insights.speaker: Henry Storey
insights.so_what: A leading indicator that China's SME base is already shifting toward higher-value services faster than other sectors.
insights.locator: Companies providing research, tech, and software services

themes.theme: Structural barriers to an Apple or Nvidia

insights.claim: China's usual industrial policy toolkit — heavy subsidies, currency management, preferential procurement and tech transfer — is not well-suited to developing producer services, unlike its effectiveness in building manufacturing capacity.
insights.speaker: Henry Storey
insights.so_what: Suggests China's proven manufacturing-subsidy playbook won't straightforwardly transfer to building design/IP/software champions — a reason for skepticism on near-term catch-up.

insights.claim: It is difficult to imagine many Chinese companies adopting Apple's fabless model of designing everything in-house while outsourcing manufacturing, because China's economic culture is built around security and self-sufficiency and an obsession with hardware and vertical integration.
insights.speaker: Henry Storey
insights.so_what: Identifies a cultural/structural mismatch, not just a policy gap, as the deeper obstacle to China producing an Apple-equivalent — relevant to long-horizon bets on Chinese tech-services champions.
insights.quote: It’s incredibly difficult to imagine many companies in China adopting Apple’s model.

insights.claim: Chinese companies aspiring to compete with Nvidia face export control restrictions and political pressure to use domestic suppliers, constraining their ability to build the kind of globally open, talent-and-capital-attracting ecosystem that companies like Apple, Nvidia and Google rely on.
insights.speaker: Henry Storey
insights.so_what: A binding external constraint (export controls) compounding the internal cultural mismatch — dual headwinds for Chinese chip-design champions specifically.
insights.locator: Even those that are trying, such as the Chinese companies aspiring to compete with Nvidia

insights.claim: Replicating the success of companies like Apple and Nvidia may test the limits of Chinese economic exceptionalism, given they are inherently global companies built on largely unfettered openness to talent, technology, capital and ideas.
insights.speaker: Henry Storey
insights.so_what: The article's bottom-line call: skepticism, on balance, that China produces a true Apple/Nvidia-equivalent global producer-services champion in the foreseeable future.
